summary: Living in Florida, I've had to deal with frequent hurricanes, occasional tornadoes, and many fires caused by lightning since it's the lightning strike capital of the United States. This environment really shaped my belief in emergency preparedness. Being in a small, somewhat rural county in Florida helped us avoid the worst of the COVID-19 spread compared to urban areas. The Sunshine State's bright sunlight also seemed to help reduce virus transmission during the pandemic.

Before transitioning, I was an Eagle Scout, and that experience deeply influenced my commitment to being prepared for emergencies. The training and mindset from scouting taught me the importance of anticipating problems and having the right gear and skills ready. This background was crucial when facing events like hurricanes and the COVID-19 pandemic, where preparedness made a big difference.

When the COVID-19 pandemic hit in early 2020, I was already well-prepared thanks to my prepping background. Living with my elderly parents in their 80s, I was especially cautious to avoid bringing the virus home, always masking and sanitizing after outings. Despite some inconveniences like toilet paper shortages, we had power, access to food delivery, and entertainment like Netflix, so it felt manageable. I also helped others through my Blue Collar Prepping blog and Facebook group by providing advice and community support during the crisis.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, having Netflix was a real comfort since we had power and could stay entertained while mostly staying home. It helped make the lockdowns and social distancing easier to bear. For me and my family, it was part of what made the pandemic a 'non-issue' compared to more severe disasters I've prepared for.

DoorDash was a lifesaver during the pandemic because it allowed us to get food delivered safely without having to go out much. Since I live with elderly parents, minimizing exposure was critical, and DoorDash helped us maintain that safety. It was part of the reason why, despite the pandemic, our experience was relatively comfortable compared to what many feared.

I curate a preparedness blog called Blue Collar Prepping, which became especially important during the COVID-19 pandemic. Along with others in the community, I used it to offer advice, emotional support, and practical tips to people feeling isolated or worried. It was rewarding to help others stay calm and prepared during such an uncertain time.

In early 2020, around February, I noticed the Democratic Party was encouraging people to go out and celebrate events like Chinese New Year, downplaying the risks of COVID-19. This struck me as contradictory to the rumors about the virus's infectiousness, and I thought it would end up causing problems. This political context helped frame my early understanding of the pandemic's seriousness.

I recall that during the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic, the Democratic Party was telling people to go out and celebrate Chinese New Year, which seemed risky given the rumors about the virus's infectiousness. This event happened right before the pandemic really took hold in the U.S., and it was part of the mixed messages people received at the time. It was a moment that made me skeptical about how seriously the threat was being taken initially.

I attended the Life, the Universe, and Everything writers symposium in Provo, Utah, around Valentine's Day weekend in 2020. That event was one of the last bits of normalcy before the pandemic changed everything. While there, I heard about a friend’s daughter getting sick, which made the threat feel more real, even though we weren’t sure if it was COVID-19 at the time.
cues: Life, the Universe, and Everything symposium

summary: I was in Provo, Utah, attending the Life, the Universe, and Everything symposium in February 2020, just as the COVID-19 pandemic was starting to become known. This trip marked the last time I experienced something close to normal before the world shut down. It was there I heard about a friend’s daughter getting sick, which brought the pandemic closer to home.
cues: Life, the Universe, and Everything symposium
